speaker: interviewer
text: --Hersch, in Sydney, Australia. This is tape five. Peter, can you tell us about this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this photograph was taken apparently on the ship, as you can see. They're all the boys. And this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Read to us what it says on the bottom. Can you see it on the camera? Oh, well, you don't have to. They-- people can read it.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- well, it says here that they're the-- the boys that--

speaker: interviewer
text: It says 11 Jewish orphans, whose parents were killed by Germans, seen on board migrant ship, Al Sudan, at Fremantle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And they-- so you were brought with a whole group of orphans.

speaker: interviewee
text: A whole group of boys. And this lady was a doctor. And she was sort of-- she was looking after us. She was-- she came with us. But this was on the way here. And if I can show you, that's me up here-- up here. That's me.

speaker: interviewer
text: It is. You lost-- you lost a bit of weight there [LAUGHS] from the other photos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- were you frightened about, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: --yes, about coming here on your own?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was-- I was-- I didn't know what was waiting for me here. You know, I was still--

speaker: interviewer
text: It had to be better than what you'd left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. Sure. But don't forget, we didn't have any money-- none of us-- arrived here penniless.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- it wasn't an easy feeling, you know? But of course, the welfare society gave us--

speaker: interviewee
text: --a few, I think it was 10 shillings or something-- pocket money, or whatever it was at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they looked after us till we all found jobs. And most of these people, unfortunately, a lot of them have died already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you keep in contact with any of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We kept in contact. And quite a lot of them are--

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Did-- what-- so what year was this? When did you come?

speaker: interviewee
text: March, I think it was-- February, March already-- March '48.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was-- I didn't know about this, that this-- this exists. Somebody found it.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was in the newspaper at the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's me. I think this was about a year after I was liberated. As you can see, I've still got the German-- the German jacket on, because when we were liberated, we got rid of our striped uniform. And we got some-- from the-- from the Germans we got some jackets that we found. I don't now how I got it. But I-- I have. And I lost my hair, so I put on a--

speaker: interviewee
text: --a hat, a cap, one of those German caps.

speaker: interviewee
text: In this photo I would have been-- I would have been about 16.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so where was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in Germany, in the-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- where, in the DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I have made copies. And I'm giving my children one of each.

speaker: interviewee
text: This one is my daughter's, actually. I gave it to her. My-- borrowed from her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, we have so few photos.

speaker: interviewee
text: --so you can see-- so you can see what--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister. That's my only-- the only survivor of the family besides me-- my sister and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she lives in Israel now.

speaker: interviewee
text: She lives in Israel. And the-- this is her husband. They married after the war. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Russia. And that's what I couldn't-- I didn't know about her for years after that she survived. I was living in Australia already, and I found out that she's alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got in touch with her. And from then on I kept in touch, of course. And-- and it helped her, because the parcels from here-- see, they had nothing there-- You were able--

speaker: interviewee
text: --absolutely nothing. So the parcels that I sent from here kept her going there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- do you see her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yeah, I see her. I-- I go to Israel quite often. And then my son Philip lived in Israel for years. And they're very, very close. And what he's-- with my niece, they're like, you wouldn't believe it-- like brothers and sisters. And she adores him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- is it hard when you see your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It's-- it's a pleasure to see her. But then we start--

speaker: interviewee
text: --talking about what has happened. And lately, she's becoming very-- she wants me to take her to Auschwitz. She wants me to take her back where we were born, so to see the place again. But we didn't talk for years about it. All of a sudden, the 50th anniversary of liberation, and so she's--

speaker: interviewer
text: As you get older, the memories get clearer, don't they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, you don't forget any of those things. What I told you today on-- on the thing, I can see everything clearly. And-- and that's why you don't think about it, otherwise it-- it becomes a nightmare.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think about-- you don't think about it--

speaker: interviewee
text: --except when I see a film, or I see anything on the-- on the television that upsets me, and then I can't sleep all night. So that's-- I suppose that's normal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. OK. OK, Peter. Tell us about this wedding photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's my wife, Rosie. And we were married in the great synagogue. Rabbi Porush married us. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- well, we celebrated our 40th wedding anniversary last year. So it's 41 years ago-- 41 years ago. And I can tell you, I wish my children and all my friends should be as happy as I am with my wife. And thank God for that. So you see, so I'm always counting my blessings.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's been a happy-- a happy marriage.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she is-- she does a lot for the community, as you know, in music. She is the musical director of the neighborhood singers. And for any communal work, whenever they need her, for URA, JCA, you name it,

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're gonna see her. Peter, us who these people are.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, these people are my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And tell us in order, starting from the left.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is-- starting from-- from left?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter-in-law. She gave me a beautiful grandchild.

speaker: interviewee
text: This one. Her name is Karen.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that one is my son, Michael--

speaker: interviewee
text: --my eldest son, who's married to Karen. And that one is my wife, Rosie. And that's my son, Philip. And that's my daughter, Rochelle. My daughter's name is after my mother who died-- Rachelle.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my son, Philip, is after my father, Ephraim. Ephraim is his Hebrew name.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my eldest son is Moshe, after my mother's father. So all are after the--

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this was taken at the 50th anniversary of liberation. We had a dinner-- 50th celebration of liberation of the camps. And my family wanted to be there with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- and there was all the other survivors were there too, was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was packed. It was a dinner, especially for the 50 years of-- of liberation of the camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'm very proud and honored that they all wanted to be there with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: --but one that we didn't want to miss. So that's very important to me--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a-- a 40th anniversary of the Holocaust survivors. We had a get together of all the Holocaust survivors at the Maccabean Hall. And this is-- they are the ships that we came out on, as you can see.

speaker: interviewer
text: And yours is the second one, isn't it? The Al Sudan.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Al Sudan. Al Sudan was the ship that I came out at. And I just thought that that's something that we should have.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you-- are there many-- have you met many other survivors in Sydney who come from around where you come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have. I have met quite a few survivors. In fact, I met a lot of survivors that were in Mauthausen, and on the-- on the death march with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was through the gatherings of the 40th anniversary, and also on the 50th anniversary.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's very important to have those.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was. In fact, one of them is a hazzan here-- is a cantor-- Michael Deutsch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, it was Michael Deutsch? And you--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- he was in the same march as me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you not realize that before?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know. I didn't realize it. But when he talked--

speaker: interviewee
text: --to us. He said that he was on that--

speaker: interviewee
text: --death march and into the-- those camps. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Does it help to talk to people?

speaker: interviewee
text: It does. I also belong to another group I call The Children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Child Survivors group, as well as the Holocaust Survivors, because we were--

speaker: interviewee
text: So there is a-- yes. It's quite a big group here of people that belong to the Child Survivors. So I go to their meetings too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for that. OK, Peter, tell us about this newspaper article.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this newspaper article is-- it's very interesting. My son, Philip, when he was bar mitzvah, I took him to Jerusalem to the bar mitzvah, because I wanted him to have a special bar mitzvah, not just--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. I didn't have a bar mitzvah. And we're-- so he had the bar mitzvah at the Wall. And when my son gave the speech, he said, [SOBBING] I'm having the bar mitzvah with him, which something very precious to me. So this article was printed. I think somebody got it from South Africa, of all places.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when he celebrated his bar mitzvah, he made that that I'm being bar mitzvahed with him at the Wall. So that's very precious to me, very-- that's what I said about my children. And this is 1972. I can see it's written--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think that comes, that was-- it was printed in South Africa--

speaker: interviewee
text: --of all places. And I think Rabbi Porush gave it to me here, that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's why I wanted you to see that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Peter, tell us about this happy photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this-- this photo, I'm so thrilled. I'm so happy. Can you imagine to have a granddaughter born, first granddaughter? And her name is Emily Sarah. And she's beautiful. And I'm so proud and so happy-- as you can see.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was last week, wasn't it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- that was last week.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sydney, on-- on the 26th of this month.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you can see. So I'm very proud, and showing off my-- my granddaughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's the picture of her, and as you can see on my face.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK, Peter, would you like to introduce us to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. First of all, my wife. This is Rosie, my darling-- [LAUGHTER] --idol of my life. And this one is my son, Michael.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my daughter, Rochelle.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my other son, Philip.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hi. OK. Well, Peter's told us his story. And now it's your turn to talk a bit about your father. He's a very special person, we've-- we've found out. Rosie, would you like to go first--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I would. Well, when I-- when I married Peter, I had this tremendous feeling that I wanted to make up for the loss of his parents and family. And over the years, I realized that no one, and nothing, could ever-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --could ever make up for such a terrible loss. Darling, I-- I wanted to give everything of myself to you. And I tried. But--

speaker: interviewee
text: But the most amazing thing of all is that out of the despair and the darkness of your childhood, that you could find inside of yourself such great love, and such great generosity of spirit. And I want to thank you for all the years of your loving, and your caring, and your devotion, and your warmth. And you know, together we've created the most incredible miracle-- our beautiful children-- and wonder of wonders, only this week, the miracle of our very own little grandchild. Thank you, darling.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for everything. You've given me everything anybody could wish to have.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's quite an act to follow. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's see if you could top that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Michael, do you want to say-- tell us something about what sort of person your-- or what sort of father, your father has been?

speaker: interviewee
text: God, well, yeah, he's just been an incredible father. I can remember oh, just-- just the amazing love, and attention, and time that he gave to me, and still gives to me. I can remember him taking me to soccer practice, and-- and-- and-- and to swimming training, and everything else under the sun. So he has been an incredible father to me. I think what amazes me about Dad, is having gone through what-- the incredible trauma that he went through, and seeing what happened to his family, I find it just almost beyond belief that he is the person he is, that his life is not twisted with bitterness, as I think mine would have been had I gone through that, had I been able to survive that. And that is incredibly inspirational to me. I personally feel, and have felt all my life, a burning anger towards the German people for what they did to my father and his family. And you know, it's-- it wasn't just the people in the camps. It was everyone in the industrial complex, and the bureaucrats, and the neighbors who looted the houses, and all that sort of stuff. But I just find it amazing that my father lives his life the way he does, and has never taught me any of that. He's just taught me love and-- and respect for other people. So I just love him enormously, and admire him unbelievably. So that's what I'm going to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Rochelle, [LAUGHS] would you like to say something about--

speaker: interviewer
text: Peter, you've got lipstick on your [LAUGHS] chin.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know you want to look beautiful.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's something from my-- from my wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, I echo what Michael had said. I think Dad is an amazing person-- has been an incredible father. I think for myself, when coming up to this interview, I was thinking about what he's offered to me personally, and-- and what I carry with me in my life, and him and-- and I think it's about courage. And it's about faith. And it's about being proud to be Jewish, knowing where I've come from, always having that. And it's about a fighting spirit.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I feel like they're amazing qualities to have passed on to me, Dad. And I'll always have them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the other thing is that I was named after my father's mother, Rachelle. And this is Ephraim, he's named after my father's father. And I feel very proud to carry my grandmother's name. And I feel like-- I feel like in some way, her spirit can live on through me, and through all of us children, and now the-- the granddaughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that even though I never got to meet them, and they don't know us, I feel like we know them, and we remember them. And we will carry them with us. And I love you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, my God. Well, it's lucky you haven't got 10 children. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, again, of course, I echo what Michael and Rochelle have said, and the-- and the words that Dad has often said to me, that-- that always sort of ring, as that you never give up. And-- so that's been a constant thread. And as I'm sure he's told you, he-- out of his family, it was he and his sister who survived. I've just been living now 10 years in Israel. So I've seen a lot of his sister and his sister's family. So I thought I'd just-- I brought some photos just-- to show-- thought I'd show you the other branch of the family in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] I don't know if we can-- we can get-- we should have read them before.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if you can pick them up. I think you've seen a picture of his sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: We've seen a picture of his sister, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this are-- this are his sister's children. Do you want to hold them, Dad? Leah and Dov.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, they're your cousins? Yes? Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're very close with them, I hear.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very close. And this is Dov's children, Svika and Adi.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is me with Leah's son, Ephie, who's also called after Dad's father.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is Leah with her daughter, Jana.

speaker: interviewer
text: That must be a new experience for you to have extended family, because you don't have any extended family in Sydney, do you-- apart from your mother's family?

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't have any from your father's family?

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's-- it must be nice to-- yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I just wanted to be-- I just wanted to sort of complete the-- family picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's fantastic. Thank you, Philip.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did your father talk much about his experiences when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: He did talk about his experiences. I think we got-- we didn't get the whole story. We've gotten bits and pieces. But certainly, we've grown up knowing what happened, and hearing some stories about the different incidents in the camps. I've certainly been aware all of my life of what's happened. But I feel like there's a lot I don't know, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So do you think it's made a difference to how you function-- you three function-- in-- in the world, having a Holocaust survivor for a father-- how you view the world and how-- other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the thing that comes through is that family is most important. That's the thing I think that Dad-- that we got from Dad.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I think, yeah, sorry, Phillip. I just wanted to-- there are some other things, too. I think the knowledge so early in life that there is such evilness in the world does affect you. It affects you in good ways actually, and in not so good ways. The not so good ways, I think for me-- for me have been that I've had difficulty in forming relationships outside the family. I have a wonderful relationship with my wife. But I've always had trouble making friends. I guess it's just something that comes out of knowing there's such danger outside when you're little, even though Dad didn't portray that. So that's been tough.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you not trust-- you find it hard to trust other peoples?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not aware of finding it hard, but that's, I think, that's the explanation for being difficult-- finding it difficult to get close to other people, other than someone I let in, like my wife. So that's-- that's a side of an-- that's a negative. On the other side, though, I have a tremendous sense of-- sort of justice and injustice. And I think that comes out of what I think happened to my father. And so I do go out of my way to look after sort of the underdog, and care for people who are in-- in trouble. And I think that has come out of this as well. So there's good things, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] Yes. Do you agree with it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I agree with that. I think when I think about the-- I mean, I think it's had a profound effect on me and the family. And I think as well, that issue of social justice, and feeling just something about a sense of the values that I hold, that come from that experience. And I-- yeah, I think that's a strength and a-- and really positive. Personally, I know that I've carried a lot of fear in my life and that-- that-- yeah, a lot of fear. Not necessarily about specific things, but I think that does come through those experiences that dad went through. And what I said earlier about him teaching me about courage and the fighting spirit, I've also learned that. So that's how I've worked with my fear. But I think that's been one of the legacies for me as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there's pros and cons. Yeah. And did-- I mean, did it used to come up at all in your everyday life? Would it come up often, the fact that he was a survivor, or issues to do with it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we would just, it was part of our everyday life, of knowing that. And it would come out in small ways.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's what I was going to say. Did you eat all your dinner? [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, that's a classic example where it would come out. If-- we were just very aware of what it meant to leave food on our plate.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it would come out in all the-- protectiveness.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know that dad has been incredibly loving, and supportive, and overprotective. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think that protectiveness comes also-- I mean, apart from who he is--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it has an added sort of dimension that comes from the experience of losing his family. And he doesn't want to lose his family.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. So do you think you'll carry that on with your new granddaughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I must say, I mean I-- I've had a very easy life, right? I've never experienced war. I've never experienced hunger. I've got a great family. I've got a great job. Life's pretty easy for me. So I think I-- I can already feel this wave of protectiveness. [LAUGHTER] And I'm hoping-- I'm hoping to combat it. Yeah, so I don't know. I'm gonna fight it. But, it's there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it-- that perhaps, indirectly, I, in some ways prevented the three children from fully expressing perhaps anger sometimes, frustration, because I was-- I was conscious always. Peter has been through so much. And I-- if-- if there were disturbances or conflict, or anything-- let's not worry dad. Don't worry dad. And I-- I've had it in-- on my heart that perhaps I played some part maybe in not allowing them to express themselves fully.

speaker: interviewee
text: In order to protect him, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I don't mean, does-- it hasn't done them too much harm. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: They don't seem to be-- [LAUGHS] too quelled.

speaker: interviewee
text: They've come through it extremely well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So is there anything else anybody would like to say before we finish the tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No, I just want to add to what we were saying earlier about the effects. I think real strength, and strength of identity, Jewish identity-- that no matter what happens in life, I know who I am. And I feel like that has been also one of the effects.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] I want to thank you all for being in the-- in the film, and thank your dad.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, can I just say one thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you so much, Ruth. You were so wonderful--

speaker: interviewee
text: --no, the way I broke down too. And to the crew, you were fantastic. I appreciate it very much. Thank you. I hope it wasn't too tough on you.
